acoustic_nodes.description: Initial slab fracture: deep low-frequency explosive crack, predominantly below 50 Hz (infrasound and VLF). Duration milliseconds to seconds per fracture event. Propagation along fracture line at 100-500 m/s. Audible across several km in still winter air. In multi-slope event: multiple fracture cracks from different directions at different delays -- acoustic staccato of geological failure. The sound of a state change.

Slide mass in motion: surface roughness broadband roar (dry powder = higher-pitched continuous; wet dense = lower-pitched irregular). Air pressure powder blast wave preceding main mass by seconds. Sub-bass ground vibration propagating as seismic energy. Multiple simultaneous slides: roar from multiple directions overlapping. Duration: 30 seconds to several minutes per slide depending on path length.

Dense-flow front arriving at stopping zone: high-amplitude impact pulse (deep short-duration boom, seismic magnitude small earthquake equivalent for large events). Debris distribution: chunks tumbling to rest, secondary collapses, crackling settling sounds. The silence after runout: sudden cessation of roar, leaving only echo decay -- the silence is itself an acoustic event.

Bowl and ridge geometry: glacially carved cirques reflect and concentrate sound inward, multiple echo returns per event, diminishing amplitude. Ridge reflection creates distant echo sequences arriving seconds after original. Multi-slope echo landscape: echo sequences from multiple avalanches simultaneously in decay, overlapping. The alpine bowl filled with geological memory of preceding events.

theory_nodes.concept: Multi-slope release conditions: (1) Widespread weak layer failure across aspects; (2) Simultaneous storm loading across multiple aspects; (3) Sympathetic triggering -- a large initial release on one slope destabilizes adjacent loaded slopes. Occurrence: a handful of times per decade per area. Rarity distinguishes the multi-slope sequence from common single-slope events (hundreds per season in Washington Cascades).
theory_nodes.description: Multi-slope release conditions -- rare enough to be the geophysical maximum, common enough to be documentable.

theory_nodes.concept: The four-phase sequential acoustic structure: fracture crack (milliseconds, infrasound impulse) → mass in motion (seconds to minutes, broadband roar + sub-bass) → runout impact (seconds, high-amplitude pulse + debris) → alpine echo decay (minutes, bowl geometry memory). Multiple releases overlapping create temporal complexity: Phase 2 of one slide simultaneous with Phase 1 of another, Phase 4 of early slides simultaneous with Phase 2 of later slides.
theory_nodes.description: Four-phase sequence -- and the complexity of multiple releases at different phases simultaneously.

theory_nodes.concept: Alpine bowl geometry as acoustic architecture: glacially carved cirques provide reflection surfaces that create multiple echo returns from single events. Ridge reflection sends acoustic energy across valleys, arriving seconds after origin. The terrain geometry is an acoustic instrument: each bowl has its own echo signature. A listener positioned correctly can hear the terrain geometry through the echo structure.
theory_nodes.description: Alpine bowl geometry -- the terrain as acoustic instrument, echo as terrain map.

theory_nodes.concept: Fracture crack infrasound: predominantly below 50 Hz, extending into infrasound. Felt as much as heard at close range. Propagates differently than audible sound: lower attenuation at distance, can be felt across kilometers as chest vibration before the audible components arrive. The seismic dimension of the fracture crack is the geological scale of the event -- the snowpack failing is a geological process.
theory_nodes.description: Fracture crack infrasound -- felt before heard, geological scale acoustic event.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Physical force maximum. TAD = wall of simultaneous sonic forces. Cascades avalanche = wall of simultaneous geophysical forces. Multi-source simultaneous maximum from multiple directions.

Both alpine Cascades maximum events. SPS-346 = full Pacific storm (meteorological maximum). SPS-350 = multi-slope avalanche (geophysical consequence of storm loading). The storm creates the conditions; the avalanche is the release.
Both alpine maximum events. Ridge wind = continuous atmospheric force. Avalanche sequence = episodic geological release. Different timescales of maximum: continuous vs instantaneous.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Sustained maximum physical force as complex multi-layered simultaneous event. TAD = downtuned guitar + bass + drums + voice all simultaneously at maximum. Cascades avalanche = fracture crack + mass-in-motion + runout impact from multiple slopes simultaneously. Both are walls -- sonic and geophysical.
